revision.text:
{{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', the first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]—[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]—ascended to the throne. The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|qq=}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]

The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|qq=}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]

The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|qq=}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', the first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]—[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]—ascended to the throne. The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|1=Act on National Holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421011}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', the first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]—[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]—ascended to the throne. The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|1=Act on National Holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421011}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]

<!--Kigensetsu--> [[Category:Pre-war Japanese public holidays]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Jimmu]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:紀元節]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11604982}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', the first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]—[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]—ascended to the throne. The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|1=Act on National Holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421011}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]
{{translated page|ja|紀元節|version=103716022|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--Kigensetsu--> [[Category:Pre-war Japanese public holidays]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Jimmu]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:紀元節]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11604982}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', the first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]—[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]—ascended to the throne. The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|1=Act on National Holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421011}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]

A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.

{{wikidata link|Q11604982}} [[Category:Emperor Jimmu]] [[Category:February]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Pre-war Japanese public holidays]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', the first [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]—[[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]—ascended to the throne. The date was set as [[:en:February 11|February 11]] in 660 BCE. It was established in [[:en:1873|1873]] (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|1=Act on National Holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421011}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In [[:en:1966|1966]] (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as [[:en:National Foundation Day (Japan)|National Foundation Day]] as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.

However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * [[:en:Japanese imperial year|Japanese imperial year]] * [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]] * [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]]
{{translated page|ja|紀元節|version=103716022|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:紀元節]][[zh:紀元節]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11604982]] Wikidata name: Kigen-setsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Special characters|explanation=[[Microsoft code page 932]] (using hashigo-taka)}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Kigensetsu''' (紀元節) was a Japanese holiday established to commemorate the day on which, according to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', the first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}—{{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}—ascended to the throne. The date was set as {{ill|February 11|en|February 11|WD=Q2335|lt=February 11}} in 660 BCE. It was established in {{ill|1873|en|1873|WD=Q7757|lt=1873}} (Meiji 6). Formerly one of the four major national holidays (''Yonjōsetsu''){{efn|The four major national holidays (Yonjōsetsu) were the old system’s four primary festival days: Kigensetsu (Feb. 11), Shihōsetsu (Jan. 1), Tenchōsetsu (the Emperor’s birthday), and Meijisetsu.}} among the old national holidays.
Following the promulgation and enforcement of the {{ill|Act on National Holidays (Japan)|ja|国民の祝日に関する法律|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Act on National Holidays (Japan)|1=Act on National Holidays|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421011}} on July 20, 1948, Kigensetsu and the other ''Yonjōsetsu'' holidays were abolished.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|author=小野雅章 |date=2021-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |title=象徴天皇制下における祝日学校儀式の展開過程 : 復古的天皇観と象徴天皇観との相克 |journal=教育學雑誌 |ISSN=02884038 |publisher=日本大学教育学会 |volume=57 |page=1 |doi=10.20554/nihondaigakukyouikugakkai.57.0_1 |id={{CRID|1390850578954444160}}}}</ref> In {{ill|1966|en|1966|WD=Q2649|lt=1966}} (Shōwa 41), the same date of February 11 was re-established as {{ill|National Foundation Day (Japan)|en|National Foundation Day (Japan)|WD=Q1059995|lt=National Foundation Day}} as a public holiday, effective the following year.
The date of February 11 was determined by converting the date in the ''Nihon Shoki''—recorded as the 1st day of the 1st month of the year Jimmu ascended the throne (660 BCE)—into the Gregorian calendar during the Meiji era.
== Until the Establishment of Kigensetsu == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
According to the early 8th-century ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Jimmu’s enthronement date was “Spring, the first month of a year of Kanoto-tori (辛酉), on the day Kō-shin (庚辰) of the new moon,” i.e., '''the 1st day of the 1st month (old lunar calendar)'''. {{Quotation|辛酉年春正月庚辰朔、天皇即帝位於橿原宮|''Nihon Shoki'', Scroll 3, Emperor Jimmu’s chronicle}}
On November 15, Meiji 5 (December 15, 1872), the Meiji government established the Jimmu Era based on Emperor Jimmu’s accession (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.342), and on the same day (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344), designated “the 29th day of the First Month” ('''January 29''') as a holiday corresponding to Emperor Jimmu’s accession day. On the Gregorian calendar, January 29, 1873 (Meiji 6) corresponded to what would have been Meiji 6, January 1 (old lunar calendar) if no calendar reform had been made. Shortly after, on December 2, Meiji 5 (December 31, 1872), it was decided that the next day would be Meiji 6, January 1 (January 1, 1873 Gregorian), adopting the solar (Gregorian) calendar.
In 1873 (Meiji 6), on January 29, ceremonies to honor Emperor Jimmu’s accession were conducted. Later in January, a proclamation was issued making Emperor Jimmu’s accession day and the Emperor’s birthday (Tenchōsetsu) public holidays. On March 7, Meiji 6, it was decreed that Jimmu’s accession day be called “Kigensetsu” (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.91).
Initially, due to previous regulations (Meiji 5 Daijōkan Proclamation No.337), festival days from the old lunar calendar were to be assigned the same month and day in the new Gregorian calendar. This created confusion among the populace, who mistook Kigensetsu for a holiday celebrating the old lunar new year (since it fell in January according to the old assignment). The government realized that the true meaning of Kigensetsu (celebrating Jimmu’s enthronement) was not taking hold among the people. Also, January 29 was close to Emperor Kōmei’s death anniversary (January 30), causing inconvenience.
On October 14, Meiji 6, the government re-determined Jimmu’s accession date, setting Kigensetsu on '''February 11''' (Meiji 6 Daijōkan Proclamation No.344). This date, calculated by the Ministry of Education’s Astronomy Bureau and verified by calendar scholar Tsukamoto Akiki, was determined based on a method involving cyclical Chinese stems and branches, ensuring that the “Kanoto-tori year, first month, Kō-shin day” aligns with February 11 in the Gregorian calendar.
== From its Establishment to Becoming “Ken koku kinen no hi” (National Foundation Day) == {{Main|National Foundation Day}}{{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
On Kigensetsu, rituals were performed in the Imperial Palace’s Koreiden (imperial ancestral shrine), and Jimmu’s mausoleum was worshipped remotely across the country. With the promulgation of the Meiji Constitution on Kigensetsu in 1889, it also became associated with the constitution’s anniversary. In 1891, regulations for ceremonies in elementary schools on national holidays and festivals included reverence toward the Emperor’s portrait, reading of the Imperial Rescript on Education, etc. From 1914, Kigensetsu festivals were performed in shrines nationwide, and from 1926 on, youth groups and veterans associations began holding “Ken koku sai” (state founding ceremonies) as well.
After World War II, in 1947, a government bill proposed “Ken koku no hi” (National Foundation Day) on the same date to suit the new constitution. The Allied GHQ removed it. In 1952, after regaining independence, movements arose to restore the holiday. In 1966, a revision to the Holiday Law established “Ken koku kinen no hi” without specifying a date in the law itself, leaving it to an ordinance. In December 1966, the Sato Cabinet designated February 11 as National Foundation Day by ordinance, effective 1967. Thus, the original Kigensetsu date became National Foundation Day.
== Kigensetsu Festival == {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
During the Kigensetsu period, a Kigensetsu Festival (Kigensetsusai) was held at the Imperial Palace’s Three Palace Sanctuaries and at shrines throughout Japan. After WWII and the abolishment of old festival regulations, the Kigensetsu Festival was no longer held as a major festival. However, starting in 1949, the Emperor performed “Rinji Gohai” (temporary worship), and from the Heisei era onward, it’s called “Sanden Gohai” with the Emperor paying respects in a manner similar to old times. With the establishment of National Foundation Day in 1967, many shrines resumed ceremonies akin to Kigensetsu. Although some groups request the revival of Kigensetsu as an imperial ceremony, the Imperial Household Agency declines. Right-wing groups still hold rallies and parades celebrating Kigensetsu on National Foundation Day in the 21st century.
== The Song “Kigensetsu” == {{Wikisource|Holiday and Festival Songs}} {{More footnotes|date=April 2024|section=1}}
A song “Kigensetsu” by Takasaki Masakaze (lyrics) and Izawa Shūji (music) was published in 1888 and chosen as a holiday festival song in 1893.
== Various Theories == Some sources (like “Kenmu Nenkan Ki” and “Kenmu Nenchū Gyoji”) suggest the choice of February 11 may have been inspired by an event on that date in the Kenmu era, where Emperor Go-Daigo performed a rite at Kan no Miya in the Imperial Palace, but details are uncertain.<ref name="shokiangou1990">Hayashi Seigo, “Nihon Shoki no Angō: The Real Ancient History,” Kodansha, 1990</ref>
== Others == On Kigensetsu, funds were granted by the Imperial Household Ministry to hospitals and charities as recognition, and prefectural authorities performed official commendations. After WWII, many such commendations continued but on different significant days.
Imperial pardons (onsha) were granted on some Kigensetsu anniversaries, for example in 1934 and 1938.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References == * Nihon Bunka Kenkyūkai, “Jinmu Tennō Kigen-ron Kigensetsu no Tada shii Mikata,” Tachibana Shobō, 1958.
== See Also == * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|en|Japanese imperial year|WD=Q1209728|lt=Japanese imperial year}} * {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|WD=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar}} * {{ill|Kashihara Shrine|en|Kashihara Shrine|WD=Q710333|lt=Kashihara Shrine}}
